common_name: Red Knight Bell Pepper (Hybrid Thick-Walled Red Bell Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting after last frost; Red Knight is harvested green at 65 days from transplant or left to ripen red at 80–85 days. The red-ripe stage has much higher vitamin C and sweetness than the green stage. In zone 9, transplant March–April for June–August harvest. Red Knight shows excellent resistance to Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV), which helps maintain productivity in summer stress conditions.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 80–85°F; transplant to full sun, well-drained soil after last frost. Red Knight is a premium F1 hybrid bell pepper from PanAmerican Seed that has become one of the most consistently recommended thick-walled, high-quality bell peppers for both home gardens and commercial production in the eastern United States — a large (4 x 4 inch), blocky, 4-lobed bell pepper with very thick walls (0.3–0.35 inch) that set it apart from thinner-walled standard bell types. The thick walls are the key eating-quality trait: they provide a satisfying crunch, substantial texture for stuffing (the walls hold up to heat without collapsing), and a longer postharvest shelf life than thin-walled types that wilt rapidly after picking. Red Knight ripens from deep, dark green to a uniformly rich red with clean, complete color change. The fruit flavor at the red stage is noticeably sweeter and more complex than at the green stage — the extended ripening time converts starches and develops sugars, creating a pepper that many cooks use specifically in the red stage for roasted red pepper, jarred pimento-style preparations, and raw salads. Disease resistance includes Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV), which can devastate pepper plantings in summer heat — a practical advantage in zone 8–10 summer production.
sources: PanAmerican Seed / Ball Horticultural — Red Knight bell pepper variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Bell pepper thick-wall comparison
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pepper Production in Florida

common_name: Giant Marconi Sweet Pepper (AAS-Winning Italian Roasting Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Giant Marconi is an Italian frying/roasting pepper type that produces best with consistent moisture and good fertility. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer and fall harvest. Harvest green for frying and grilling, or allow to ripen red for sweeter roasting and antipasto use. The very large, thick-walled fruits hold up exceptionally well to heat for frying, stuffing, and grilling.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 80–85°F; transplant to full sun, fertile, well-drained soil. Giant Marconi is a 1997 All-America Selections-winning Italian sweet frying/roasting pepper that produces some of the largest and most productive sweet non-bell peppers available to home gardeners — long (7–10 inch), slightly tapered, blocky-shouldered fruits with very thick flesh and virtually no heat. The distinctive form is characteristic of Italian frying peppers: longer and more tapered than bell peppers, with a ridged, slightly irregular exterior and a meaty, thick-walled interior with surprisingly few seeds relative to total volume. The large, thick-walled fruits are extraordinary for roasting and grilling — the thick flesh holds its structure as the skin blisters and chars, and after peeling, the roasted flesh is silky, sweet, and rich with concentrated pepper flavor. Giant Marconi is the ideal choice for Italian peperonata (braised peppers with olive oil and garlic), stuffed peppers, antipasto, and any application calling for large quantities of roasted sweet pepper. The plants are tall and productive, often requiring staking when heavily loaded; they produce heavily from midsummer through fall in zone 9. The very sweet, mild flavor at the red-ripe stage is excellent raw in salads and on cheese boards where the visual impact of the large, vivid red fruits is attractive.
sources: All-America Selections — Giant Marconi pepper AAS winner 1997
Burpee Seeds — Giant Marconi variety notes
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Pepper Production

common_name: Hungarian Sweet Wax Pepper (Banana Pepper, Sweet Yellow Wax Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; harvest Hungarian Sweet Wax at the waxy-yellow stage (65–70 days) for pickling and fresh use, or allow to ripen to orange and red for a sweeter flavor. The sweet wax type is distinct from the hot wax type (Hungarian Hot Wax) — both look similar at the yellow stage but the sweet type has virtually no heat. Harvest frequently to encourage continued production.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 78–85°F; transplant to full sun after last frost. Hungarian Sweet Wax (also commonly called banana pepper, though this term covers both sweet and hot wax types) is one of the most widely grown pickling peppers in North America — a long (5–7 inch), tapered, waxy-skinned, pale yellow to golden-yellow pepper with very mild to no heat that has become the standard home pickle pepper for generations of American gardeners. The waxy, smooth skin characteristic gives these peppers excellent structure for pickling — the thin walls absorb brine quickly, the skin maintains its integrity through the canning process, and the mild flavor showcases the pickling brine without competition from pepper heat. Hungarian Sweet Wax is the standard banana pepper of pizza restaurants and deli sandwiches across the United States, where it appears in countless jar formats — whole, sliced into rings, or pickled in brine. Beyond pickling, the fresh peppers are excellent eaten raw (sweeter and crisper than bell peppers), stuffed with cream cheese, and sliced in salads. Prolific plants produce heavily throughout the summer, yielding more peppers per plant than bell types. The sweet type should not be confused with Hungarian Hot Wax (which has 1,000–15,000 SHU); both look nearly identical at the yellow harvest stage.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Hungarian Sweet Wax pepper variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pepper Production Guide
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Pepper Variety Evaluation

common_name: Lipstick Sweet Pepper (Pimiento-Type Mini Sweet Bell Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Lipstick is a compact plant (24 inches) bearing 3–4 inch pimiento-type fruits that ripen from green to deep, vivid red. Unlike most bell peppers, Lipstick ripens to red relatively quickly (70–80 days) without the extended 3-week+ green-to-red window of larger types. In zone 9, transplant March–April; plants often continue producing into fall. Lipstick does not require as much fertilizer as standard bell types — moderate fertility produces best flavor.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 80–85°F; transplant to full sun after last frost with spacing of 15–20 inches. Lipstick is a Burpee-developed pimiento-type sweet pepper that produces small to medium (3 x 3.5 inch), heart-shaped, very thick-walled fruits of the pimiento class — a pepper type characterized by a rounder, more compact form than standard bell peppers, extraordinarily thick walls relative to total size, very few seeds, and exceptional sweetness. The pimiento type has been prized by Spanish and Mediterranean cooks for centuries for its superior eating quality (pimientos are what give pimento-stuffed olives their distinctive flavor and the Spanish name 'pimiento' itself means pepper in Spanish). Lipstick delivers the classic pimiento character in a compact, prolific plant suitable for small gardens and containers: the fruits have very thick walls that hold up to heat, an extremely sweet, mild flavor with no heat, and the characteristic heart-shaped cross-section when sliced that makes them visually distinctive from bell peppers. The vivid, saturated red color at full ripeness is striking and the thick walls make Lipstick excellent for roasting, stuffing with cheese, and eating raw where the thickness provides a satisfying crunch. Ripens more completely and uniformly than many larger bell types, making it practical for gardeners who want to harvest primarily red-ripe peppers.
sources: Burpee Seeds — Lipstick sweet pepper variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Pimiento-type pepper comparison
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Pepper Variety Evaluation

common_name: Keystone Resistant Giant Pepper (Pennsylvania Heirloom Stuffing Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting after last frost; Keystone Resistant Giant is an open-pollinated heirloom that tends to produce more large fruits when staked or caged. In zone 9, transplant March–April for a June–September harvest. The 'Resistant' in the name refers to Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV) resistance — important in an era before comprehensive disease resistance packages in peppers.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 75–85°F; transplant to full sun with consistent moisture and balanced fertility. Keystone Resistant Giant is a Pennsylvania-origin heirloom bell pepper that was one of the most important commercial bell pepper varieties in the eastern United States from the 1940s through the 1970s, valued as the largest-fruited TMV-resistant open-pollinated bell pepper available during a period when disease resistance was achieved only through strain selection rather than modern breeding techniques. The 'Keystone' name connects the variety to Pennsylvania (the Keystone State), where it was grown extensively by commercial growers, home gardeners, and the Pennsylvania Dutch communities who prize large, thick-walled peppers for stuffing with meat and rice. The fruits are distinctly large (4–5 inches tall, 3–4 inches wide), blocky, 3-4 lobed, dark green ripening to red, with moderately thick walls and an upright, pendant orientation on strong-stemmed plants. Keystone Resistant Giant represents the pre-F1-hybrid era of American pepper breeding and occupies an important place in American heirloom pepper history — it was the standard against which American bell peppers were measured for decades and remains a valued open-pollinated option for seed savers who want a true-breeding large stuffing pepper. The flavor at the red stage is rich, sweet, and classically bell-pepper, with none of the bitterness that characterizes some heirloom types.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Keystone Resistant Giant variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Pennsylvania heirloom pepper collection
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Historical Bell Pepper Variety Documentation
